title: Urban wetlands as hotspots of antibiotic resistomes and their potential viral transmission
data_sources: Urban raw sewage / sewage effluent datasets

data_scope.key_comparison: ARG abundance ~9x higher than natural lakes, comparable to raw urban sewage
data_scope.time_window: not stated in public metadata (Methods only)

core_conclusion: Across 17 urban wetlands in China, antibiotic resistance genes occur at average abundances about nine times higher than in natural lakes and comparable to raw urban sewage, making these green-infrastructure wetlands resistome hotspots. They harbor human bacterial pathogens and horizontal-gene-transfer signals, and ARG-carrying phages may spread resistance through bacterial communities. Higher economic development is associated with lower ARG abundance, implying sewer-infrastructure quality shapes the geography of resistance; stormwater should be treated before reaching wetlands.
why_it_matters: Reframes flood-sponge wetlands as a public-health liability, names a viral/horizontal transmission mechanism, links resistance to socioeconomic and sewer-infrastructure gradients, and yields a concrete intervention (treat stormwater before discharge).
why_nature: Couples urban form to microbial and human/wildlife health under a One Health frame, benchmarks against global lake and sewage datasets for a transferable claim, and is directly policy-translatable for sponge-city and blue-green-infrastructure programs.
added_value_to_my_research: Repeat metagenomic sampling + STKDE -> spatiotemporal resistome hotspot surveillance grid
Explainable ML + MGWR on ARG drivers -> place-specific, spatially non-stationary resistance risk map
Phage-host bipartite graph -> SIR / agent-based contagion model of ARG transfer
Exposure-weighted resistome surface -> negative-accessibility R2SFCA for environmental hazard exposure
City water-waste flux + facility-location -> efficiency-equity siting of runoff treatment to cut ARG load
